figures.caption: FIG. 7. (a) Temperature dependences of electric resistivity. Anisotropy between ρxx and ρyy was weak. (b) The magnetic field dependence of ρxx for H ‖ b. The metamagnetic transitions induce the abrupt anomaly in ρxx.

FIG. 1. (a) Orthorhombic crystal structure of Ce2PtGe6. The space group was determined to be Cmce. (b) Magnetic structure of Ce2PtGe6 determined in this study. The propagation vector is q = 0, and the magnetic point group is represented by m′m′m, which allows AHE. This structure consists of the large collinear AFM component along the b-axis (1.7 µB) and the negligibly small FM component along the c-axis (0.001 µB).

TABLE I. Structural parameters of Ce2PtGe6 and Ce2CuGe6, determined by single-crystal X-ray diffraction measurements at T = 293 K. Ce2PtGe6 was grown by the self-flux method, while Ce2CuGe6 was obtained by the Bi-flux method.

FIG. 8. (a) Field dependence of ρyx of Ce2PtGe6 for H ‖ c. The clear hysteresis appears below TN and nonzero ρyx remains at zero field. (b) Temperature dependence of the coercive field, Hc for Ce2TGe6 (T = Cu, Pd, and Pt). The Hc for T = Pt significantly increases with decreasing temperature in contrast to other two compounds. (c) Temperature dependence of the anomalous Hall resistivity, ρAH, for Ce2TGe6 (T = Pt, Pd, and Cu). The sign of ρAH was defined as that obtained when the positive magnetic field was reduced to zero. The largest |ρAH| was obtained for T = Pt. (d) Temperature dependence of the AHC for three compounds. In high-quality Ce2CuGe6, both intrinsic and extrinsic contribution make the two-step temperature dependence [12]. σAH ≃ 300 Ω−1cm−1 was obtained for Ce2PtGe6. The solid lines indicate σAH ∼ MAF(T ), which are assumed as the intrinsic contribution. (e) The scaling relation of the M̃ · |σAH| versus σ, where M̃ = MAF(0)/MAF(T ).

FIG. 2. (a) Temperature dependence of magnetic susceptibility for H ‖ a, b, and c. The clear kink at TN = 9.2 K for H ‖ b indicates the AFM transition. (b) Temperature dependence of magnetization for H ‖ c at 0.02 T. The increase in magnetization below TN suggests that a small FM component is involved in the ordered state. The spontaneous magnetizations obtained from the hysteresis loop are also plotted (orange circles). The magnetization decreases towards low temperatures. (c) Field dependence of magnetization at several temperatures. In the ordered state, weak spontaneous magnetization with hysteresis appears. The coercive force increases at low temperatures, and reaches almost 5 T at 4.2 K. At 3 K, the maximum field of the experimental setup was not sufficient to reverse the magnetic domain. The data were taken after the field-cooling in both positive and negative fields. The spontaneous magnetization clearly decreases with decreasing temperature. (d) The field dependence of the magnetization at 2 K. The metamagnetic transitions were observed at approximately 1 T for H ‖ b. (e) The temperature dependences of the metamagnetic transition for H ‖ b. The plotted data were obtained during the field-decreasing process. They show small hysteresis with widths of less than 0.05 T. (f) The magnetic phase diagram for H ‖ b. The AFM I corresponds to zero-field AFM state. The AFM II phase emerges in the narrow field region.

FIG. 4. (a) Temperature dependence of peak profiles of the magnetic reflection (0 0 1). Solid curves show the two-gaussian fitting curves. Line scan along the (b) (0 0 L) and (c) (H 0 1) at 1.8 K. Red arrows represent nuclear reflections and blue ones represent magnetic reflections in the panels (b) and (c). (d) Intensities of magnetic reflections accessible in the present experimental setup. Red data represent the observed intensities, while data in other colors represent the calculated intensities based on symmetrically independent magnetic structures with the given magnetic point groups (MPG). All data are normalized to the strongest intensity in each model.
